coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent folic acid-chitosan/carbon dot for pH-responsive drug delivery and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kariminia, Samira
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, one of the most important reasons of death in the world is cancer. With the development of nanotechnology, advanced methods for treatment of cancer have introduced. In this work, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from chitosan as the second abundant polysaccharide present in the nature. The surface of CDs was modified with chitosan (CDs/CS) and then the amino groups of chitosan were conjugated with activated folic acid (CDs/CS-FA) for controlled delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) as anticancer drug against HeLa cancer cells. The DOX loading efficiency of fluorescent CDs/CS-FA was high and nearly 60 %. Due to pH sensitive swelling/deswelling of CS, the percentage of cumulative DOX release could reach 90 % at cancer tissue (pH of 5.0) and 52 % at normal tissue (pH of 7.4) within 30 h. The cytotoxicity study revealed that the synthesized CDs were highly compatible on HeLa cells with cell viability 97–88 %. Cellular imaging shows that the entry of CDs/CS-FA to HeLa cells causes a green fluorescence, while the CDs/CS without FA have a negligible fluorescence. These results are due to the important role of FA in cell internalization. Thus, the CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier is suitable candidate for controlled pH sensitive drug delivery and cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Chitosan
Folic acid
Doxorubicin
HeLa cells
Drug delivery
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent pH-sensitive CS-coated CDs (CS/CDs)
Loading of DOX onto CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier
TEM and DLS analysis of CDs and CDs/CS
Photostability of CDs under xenon (Xe) lamp irradiation
Analytical studies of DOX loading on CDs/CS-FA
Effect of various drug concentrations on loading efficiency of CDs/CS-FA
Effect of different pH values on DOX loading efficiency of CDs/CS-FA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) CS and CDs, (b) CDs, CDs/CS, CD/CS-FA, free DOX and DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier.

Fluorescence emission spectra (with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm in 10 nm increments) of the (a) CDs and (b) DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA (inset of figure (a): dialyzed CDs in room and UV light).
(a) and (b) TEM image and particle size distribution of the CDs, (c) and (d) TEM image and particle size distribution of the CDs/CS.
XRD patterns of (a) CS and (b) CDs and (c) effect of pH variations on the zeta potential of CDs/CS-FA solution.
FTIR spectra of (a) CS, (b) CDs, (c) CDs/CS, (d) CDs/CS-FA and (e) DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA.
The full scan and deconvoluted XPS peaks of CDs.

The fluorescent stability of CDs: a) photostability of prepared CDs and fluorescein (10 ppm) irradiated by a 450 W xenon lamp in different times, (b) the fluorescence spectra of CDs and (c) the fluorescence spectra of fluorescein at different times (0 to 140 min).
Variation of loading efficiency with (a) different concentrations of DOX and (b) different pH values of CDs/CS-FA solution.
In vitro release of DOX from CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier at 37 °C in PBS buffer (pH 7.4 and 5.0).
Mathematical models of DOX release from CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier.

Viability of HeLa cells after incubation with different concentrations of CDs, CDs/CS, DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA and free DOX. Data are represented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) for the three experiments.
Fluorescence microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with (a) CDs/CS-FA nonocarrier and (b) CDs/CS without FA (magnification of ×10).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent folic acid-chitosan/carbon dot for pH-responsive drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, one of the most important reasons of death in the world is cancer. With the development of nanotechnology, advanced methods for treatment of cancer have introduced.

In this work, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from chitosan as the second abundant polysaccharide present in the nature. The surface of CDs was modified with chitosan (CDs/CS) and then the amino groups of chitosan were conjugated with activated folic acid (CDs/CS-FA) for controlled delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) as anticancer drug against HeLa cancer cells. The DOX loading efficiency of fluorescent CDs/CS-FA was high and nearly 60 %. Due to pH sensitive swelling/deswelling of CS, the percentage of cumulative DOX release could reach 90 % at cancer tissue (pH of 5.0) and 52 % at normal tissue (pH of 7.4) within 30 h. The cytotoxicity study revealed that the synthesized CDs were highly compatible on HeLa cells with cell viability 97–88 %. Cellular imaging shows that the entry of CDs/CS-FA to HeLa cells causes a green fluorescence, while the CDs/CS without FA have a negligible fluorescence. These results are due to the important role of FA in cell internalization. Thus, the CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier is suitable candidate for controlled pH sensitive drug delivery and cellular imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanotechnology has been used to produce and develop carriers for the treatment of various types of cancers [1]. There are different types of nanostructures such as nanoparticles, liposomes, dendrimers, nanogels, carbon based nanoparticles, etc. for delivering the drug to the specific cancerous area [2,3].

An ideal carrier for drug delivery should have following characteristics: high stability, nontoxicity, good mechanical strength, good biocompatibility, ease of drug loading and controlled release of the drug. However, the mentioned nanocarriers do not have all the features, and the loading of the drug in these nanostructures is still not clear [4].

As a new member of carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs), with size range <10 nm, have gained interest in bio-imaging and drug delivery applications [5,6]. They have unique features such as water solubility, excitation-dependent emission, good biocompatibility, unique optical properties and simple functionalization. These properties of CDs makes them superior to the commonly used toxic quantum dots (QDs) like CdS, CdTe, and CdSe [7,8]. Also, high specific surface area, π-π stacking, and many functional groups of the CDs increases the efficiency of drug loading. Because of their fluorescence and high quantum yield, CDs are excellent candidates for fluorescence bio-imaging and real-time monitoring of drug distribution [9–12].

There are a variety of methods for fabrication of fluorescent CDs such as, laser ablation of graphite [13], microwave mediated synthesis [14,15], thermal decomposition of organic compounds [16,17] oxidation of candle soot [18] and electro-oxidation of graphite [19].

Furthermore, in the few reports, natural plant materials were used as carbon source for fabrication of CDs. Simple synthesis from non-toxic precursors such as carbohydrates with many functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups, increases the chance of CDs binding to drug molecules in biomedical applications [20–22].

Recently, due to biocompatibility, biodegradability, nontoxicity and cationic properties, chitosan-based materials have received increasing consideration in biomedical applications [23–25]. Chitosan (CS), a linear aminopolysaccharide, is the second abundant polysaccharide present in nature. Recently, CDs were synthesized from chitosan gel by Chowdhury et al. [26]. In the present work, the CDs were prepared from CS. Afterward, they were coated with CS. The amine groups in CS have pKa of about 6.5 [27]. Thus CS can be acted as a pH-sensitive “gatekeeper” and used for designing a pH-responsive drug delivery system [28,29]. Positively charged CS could attach to mammalian cells, which is helpful for targeting cancer cells [30]. For navigation the CDs/CS, folic acid (FA), as a targeting ligand, is used. FA has high tendency to bind to folic acid receptors (FR) that are existed on different types of tumor cell membrane [28].
Besides, CS dissolves in acidic mediums, so the solubility of hydrophobic anti-cancer drugs such as doxorubicin can be increased by using protonated CS and their derivatives [31].

Doxorubicin (DOX), an anticancer drug with a broad spectrum of action, has received much attention in the whole world for more than forty years. Due to its low cancer selectivity, the therapeutic effects of DOX has limited, which causes toxicity in normal and cancer cells. Also, effectiveness of DOX in medical application is reduced because of its hypersensitivity and cardiotoxicity [32,33].

Therefore, it is necessary to develop a useful and efficient system for controllable delivery DOX to the body. In vitro release data of DOX from synthesized nanocarrier were studied and fitted with various kinetic models to find the mechanisms of drug release. More importantly, the impact of the DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier on viability of HeLa cells and bio-imaging was studied.

CS (low molecular weight, degree of deacetylation ≥85 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl) and doxorubicin (DOX) were obtained from Merck. The used chemicals are completely pure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent pH-sensitive CS-coated CDs (CS/CDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescent pH-sensitive CS/CDs were synthesized from CS as the single precursor through a two-step hydrothermal method.
Step 1: Synthesis of fluorescent CDs

In the first step, CDs were synthesized from CS according to the method described early [34,35]. Briefly, 0.5 g CS was dissolved in 20 ml 1 % acetic acid. This solution was transferred into an autoclave reactor heated at a constant temperature of 220 °C for 5 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min and passed from 0.2 μm membrane filter to remove the large particles, and then dialyzed against deionized-water with a dialysis bag (MW cut off 5 kD) for 24 h under stirring conditions to remove the additional precursors and a solution with brownish yellow color.
Step 2: Functionalization of the prepared CDs with CS (CDs/CS)

In the second step to provide the fluorescent pH-sensitive CS/CDs, 0.1 g of CS was dissolved in 20 ml of 1 % acetic acid solution. Once a clear solution was obtained, the fluorescent CDs prepared in first step were added, and after complete stirring, it was transferred into the autoclave reactor and heated at 100 °C for 4 h. The obtained solution was again dialyzed to remove the unreacted precursors. The final solution was freeze-dried and its stock solution was stored in refrigerator for further application.

0.1 g of CS was added in acetic acid solution (1 %) and dissolved. Then, the prepared CDs was mixed with solution of CS. It was then hold at 100 °C for 2 h. The settled precipitate was later washed and dried at 60 °C.

Carboxylic acid groups of FA activated to conjugate to the surface of CS [36]. At first, FA was dissolved in PBS buffer (10 ml, 5 mM, pH 7.4). For activation of carboxylic acid groups of FA, 15 mg of EDC and 25 mg of NHS were mixed with FA solution and stirred for 5 h at room temperature. In the next step, 2 ml of activated FA was added to 8 ml of CDs/CS solution and maintained for 24 h. Resultant solution was dialyzed against distilled water for 10–12 h to remove unbound FA and then dried at 60 °C. The product was then dispersed in distilled water and studied spectrophotometrically.

The quantum yields (QY) measurements of CDs was performed using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference sample with QY of 58 %. The absorption intensity of solutions was kept below 0.1 to reduce the re-absorption effects. The QY value was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
QY s
=
QY r
∗
I s
I r
∗
η s 2
η r 2
∗
A r
A s
where, I and A are respectively the intensity of the fluorescence and absorption of CDs (s) and reference solutions (r), and η is the reflective index of solutions [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of DOX onto CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier
ce:section.ce:para:
DOX loading was performed by simply mixing DOX solution with CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier. The effect of various pH values and different weight ratios of nanocarrier to DOX was studied. 1.0 mg.ml−1 of CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier with different pH values was mixed with various concentrations of DOX solution and stirred for overnight at room temperature. At first, the color of solution was yellow, and then after DOX loading changed to a color of bright orange. Then for removing unbound DOX, the above solution was dialyzed against distilled water for 12 h. The unbound DOX concentration was determined spectrophotometerically at 485 nm. The drug loading efficiency (LE) was determined using following equation:
% LE =
total amount of drug − amount of unbound drug
× 100
total amount of drug

The in vitro release behavior of prepared nanocarrier was investigated by the dialysis method. First 3 ml of the DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier was put in a dialysis bag (MW cut off 3500 Da). The dialysis bag was then placed in release medium at 37 °C under mild shaking. As the release medium, different buffer solutions with pH of 5.0 and pH of 7.4 were used. At desired time interval, 1 ml of release solution was removed and replaced with 1 ml fresh solution with same pH. The concentration of DOX in the release solution was determined at 485 nm using a calibration curve. Using suitable kinetic models, release data was analyzed and investigated [6].

The cytotoxicity of the CDs, CDs/CS-FA, DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier and free DOX was assessed using the MTT assay. HeLa cells were cultivated in 96 well plate. Afterwards, each of the CDs, CDs/CS-FA, DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier and free DOX were administered to the cells at doses of (4–32 ppm) and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Then, 20 μl of MTT solution (5 mg ml−1 in PBS) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. The supernatant containing MTT was eliminated from wells and then, 100 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to them for dissolving formazan crystal. At the end, the absorbance of the solution was read at 570 nm. The experiments were repeated three times.

The HeLa cells were seeded and incubated with CDs/CS and the CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier for 2 h at 37 °C. After this time, the cells were washed using DMSO to eliminate the adsorbed nanocarriers on the cell membrane. The cells were mixed with 2 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min and used for imaging.

Absorption spectra were measured on an UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453). Fluorescence spectra was recorded on a FLS920 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh, England). TEM image was measured with a TecnaiG2 F30 (FEI, USA) having copper grids coated with carbon. XRD patterns of the chitosan and CDs were recorded using x-ray diffractometer (XRD, Philips, the Netherlands) with Kα radiation of Cu. The FTIR spectra were recorded on FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, ALPHA, Germany) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The sizes of CDs were characterized by DLS (PCS, Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd). The cellular images were obtained with a fluorescence microscope (micros, Austria). pH measurements were carried out with a pH/ion-meter (Metrohm 713, Switzerland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded by an ESCALAB MK-II spectrometer (VG Scientific Ltd., UK).

The fluorescent CDs were synthesized from CS solution when subjected to autoclave heating. Due to formation of CDs, the colorless solution turned into a dark brown and showed a bright green fluorescence peak under UV light (λ = 365 nm) [38,39]. The QY of CDs was calculated to be 27 %.Based on the proposed hypotheses by previous researchers, structural defects cause fluorescence in CDs [40]. Furthermore, the existence of some functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxides and carboxylic acid groups on CDs due to the generation of an electron hole, have an important role in creation of fluorescence under UV light [41]. The color of the prepared CDs solution is crude, which is due to the presence of impurities and other carbonaceous materials. For this reason, the CDs solution must be purified for drug delivery applications. Dialysis method was used for the purification of crude solution of CDs against distilled water.

Absorption spectra of CS and CDs were compared and shown in Fig. 1a. Pure CS showed no considerable absorption, in while the synthesized CDs appeared two peaks at 218 and 287 nm illustrating π to π* and n to π* electron transitions associated with CC and CO groups [42–44]. For the efficient use of CDs as drug delivery vehicles and for increasing drug attachment efficiency, the surface of the CDs was modified and functionalized with CS as an ideal linker (CDs/CS).

As shown in Fig. 1b, after the surface modification, the absorption peak at 218 nm disappeared and intensity of peak at 287 nm was decreased in comparison to pure CDs, indicating the successful coating of CDs with CS. With attachment of FA on CDs/CS, background absorption was increased and a broad peak at 370 nm was appeared.

After DOX loading on CDs/CS-FA, appearance of peaks in 256 and 485 nm that are characteristic for DOX, indicating the formation of DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA. This is due to physical interactions between CS and DOX. Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs and DOX loaded CD/CS-FA excited at different wavelengths (300 to 400 nm). The intensity of fluorescence spectra at different wavelengths was varied. A sharp peak at 400 nm (λex = 330 nm) was selected due to its high fluorescence intensity.

It is believed that the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 2a) is due to their quantum size and structural defects (sp2 and sp3 carbons with specific ratios) [26]. In Fig. 2b, after the surface modification, the intensity of fluorescence spectra of CDs/CS-FA was lower than the pure CDs, indicating the formation of CDs/CS [45,46]. Also, two fluorescence peaks appeared at 500–700 nm, confirming conjugation of DOX to the surface of CDs/CS-FA. The solution of CDs was clear and the color of the solution is light brown in normal light and also, bright green fluorescence was produced under UV light (λ = 365 nm) (inset of Fig. 2a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and DLS analysis of CDs and CDs/CS
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and DLS analyses were used to investigate the morphology and size of CDs and CDs/CS. TEM analysis (Fig. 3a) shows that CDs are spherical and uniform in shape. Hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs is determined about 2 nm by DLS analysis (Fig. 3b). Also, based on TEM analysis (Fig. 3c), with coating of CDs with CS, their spherical and uniform shape are maintained but have greater size than bare CDs. This is due to successful coating of CDs with CS. As shown in Fig. 3d, hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs/CS was increased to nearly 4 nm. The results of TEM and DLS are very close and confirm each other.

XRD pattern of CS in Fig. 4a showed an amorphous peak at 20° [47]. XRD pattern of prepared CDs (Fig. 4b) showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24°, which was related to carbon atoms with an irregular structure.

Zeta potentials of CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier as a function of pH were displayed in Fig. 4c. The amine groups in CS have pKa of about 6.5, therefore at pHs lower than pKa, CS has cationic nature and the zeta potential of CDs/CS-FA at pH 5.0 was +25.0 mV. But at pHs close to pKa (pH 7.0), due to the deprotonation of CS, zeta potential decreased to became +3.5 mV. Finally, at pHs upper than pKa, because of complete deprotonation of CS polymer, the zeta potential value decreases to nearly 0.0 mV [27,48]. The obtained values for zeta potential indicate the successful coating of CDs with CS.

FT-IR spectra of CS, CDs and modified CDs were displayed in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5a, CS appeared the specific peaks of OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3458 cm−1, CH stretching vibration at 2922 and 2877 cm−1, NH bending vibration at 1643 and 1603 cm−1, CC, CN vibrations at 1258–1450 cm−1, CH bending vibration of pyranose ring at 1157 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1093 cm−1 and pyranose ring stretching vibration at 664 cm−1 [49,50]. For the CDs (Fig. 5b), the intensity of OH and NH stretching vibration at 3428 cm−1 were reduced. However, the CH vibrations (stretching and bending) of pyranose ring in CS were vanished. The observed differences in the spectra of CS and CDs show that the polymeric chains and pyranose ring of CS has been decomposed due to the process of carbonization [51]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the characteristic peaks of CS appeared in spectrum of CDs/CS, confirming coating of CDs with CS.

FA coated CDs/CS could be synthesized by chemically linking FA to CS via EDC as a carboxyl activating agent. The CDs/CS-FA spectrum (Fig. 5d) displays a band at 1650 cm−1 related to CONH amide and a band at 1575 cm−1 correspond to NH bending in the second amine at. In the pure FA and CDs/CS-FA, the absorption band at 1450 cm−1 was related to the CC stretching vibration in the aromatic ring [52]. The band at 1692 cm−1 assigned to carboxylic acid groups (COOH) of FA. Based on literature, pure DOX displays various peaks at 1071 cm−1 (CO), 1414 (CC), 1623 cm−1 and 1583 (NH), 1730 (C=O) [28]. After DOX loading (Fig. 5e), the specific bands at 1623 and 1583 cm−1 in free DOX were appeared in the spectrum of DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA and shifted to 1614 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1. Also, the major specific bands of FA, CS and CDs/CS-FA were seen, suggesting that DOX loading in CDs/CS-FA was successful. The band of stretching vibration of CO in DOX appeared at 1717 cm−1. Stretching vibration band of N − H in free DOX at 3320 cm−1 shifted to 3347 cm−1 for the DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier.

Fig. 6 shows the XPS measurements that were performed for the surface elemental analysis of CDs. The full-scan spectrum reveals the presence of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s) peaks, respectively at 284.5, 400.2, and 532.0 eV. In the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of carbon (C1s) three peaks at 284.1, 285.7, and 287.6 eV can be assigned to bonds of CC, C–N/C–O, and CO, respectively. Deconvolution of oxygen peak (O1s) display two peaks at 532.0 and 535.2 eV, corresponded to C–OH/C–O–C and CO, respectively. The nitrogen peak (N1s) deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.7 and 401.5 eV, suggesting nitrogen as CN and NH bonds, redpevtivelly. These results reveal the excistance of a variety of polar groups including hydroxyl, alkyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs.

Although many fluorescent nanomaterials have high fluorescence, they have limited applications due to their instability. For biomedical applications, one of the important features of CDs is their high stability [53]. To determine stability over time, CDs were stored in the dark for four-week at room temperature. The obtained results showed that the CDs were stable during the one-month storage period. They show no precipitation or phase separation and their optical properties were unchanged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of CDs under xenon (Xe) lamp irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: For photostability investigation, the prepared CDs and fluorescein were irradiated by 450 W xenon (Xe) lamp for 140 min. The fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs were determined and compared with fluorescence intensity of fluorescein with same concentration at λex = 350 nm. As shown in in Fig. 7a and b, no significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed for CDs, and keep ~95 % of the initial intensity even after 140 min irradiation, while the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein is greatly reduced. The fluorescence spectra of CDs and fluorescein at different times (0 to 140 min) were shown in Fig. 7b and c. These result shows that CDs have high photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical studies of DOX loading on CDs/CS-FA
ce:section.ce:para: Drug loading was studied at different ratios of the nanocarrier to drug and various pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various drug concentrations on loading efficiency of CDs/CS-FA
ce:section.ce:para: For determining the highest drug loading efficiency, the concentration of DOX gradually was increased, while the concentration of CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier was kept constant (0.1 mg.ml−1). The loading efficiencies of DOX with concentrations of 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ppm were 45 %, 60 %, 74 %, 63 % and 42 %, respectively. The highest value of drug loading efficiency was 74 % that it was obtained from DOX concentration of 60 ppm (Fig. 8a). This results also show that when the concentration of DOX increased up to 60 ppm, the aggregation effect of DOX was more obvious; consequently, the loading efficiency decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different pH values on DOX loading efficiency of CDs/CS-FA
ce:section.ce:para: DOX loading characteristics of CDs/CS-FA in various pH values (from 2.0 to 9.0) were investigated. As seen in Fig. 8b, the pH value of solution had remarkable effect on DOX loading efficiency. DOX has a single ionizable amine with pKa of 8.3 [54]. As a result, in acidic and neutral environments, DOX is protonated and in alkaline environment, it is neutral. The maximum DOX loading efficiency (62 %) occurred at pH 6.0 while concentration of DOX was kept constant (50 ppm). In acidic environments, due to repulsive force between the NH3 + groups on CDs/CS-FA and DOX, the number of hydrogen bonds between them decreased. Consequently, the amount of DOX loaded onto CS/CDs-FA nanocarrier was decreased.

At neutral pH, DOX molecules tend to be strongly adsorbed to CS. This is probably due to the weaker repulsive forces between DOX and CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier in neutral environment compared to acidic environment. When the electrostatic repulsive force between deprotonated CS and DOX is the weakest, the strongest hydrogen bond may be formed. In basic environment, both CS and DOX molecules are in their neutral state. In this system, at higher pH levels, the drug loading efficiency decreased due to self-assembling of CS in CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier.

As it is clear, in the human body, the pH of tumor tissue (5.0 or 6.0) is lower than the pH of normal tissues (7.4). Thus, release behavior of DOX was studied at pH of 5.0 and 7.4. As shown in Fig. 9 , release behavior of DOX was sustained and pH-responsive. The percentage of cumulative DOX release was increased with decreasing pH value and were 90 and 52 % at pH 5.0 and 7.4 within 30 h, respectively. It can be clearly seen that the DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA released DOX more favorably at acidic pH condition as compared to physiological pH.

Two important factors affect this phenomenon: (1) at pH 5.0, amino groups in CS and DOX were protonated (NH3 +) and the solubility of DOX increased. Thus, due to electrostatic repulsion between DOX and the nanocarrier with positive charges, the interaction between CDs/CS-FA and DOX is weakened and DOX is dissociated; (2) the high tendency of drug molecules to aggregate at pH 5.0.
The results show that CDs/CS-FA had less toxicity for normal tissue, showing safe delivery system. It was found that DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA had long, sustained, targeted-released properties [34,37].

As shown in Fig. 10 , different kinetics models including zero order, first order, Higuchi and Korsmeyer−Peppas model were used to understand mechanism of DOX release at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4. Based on the highest correlation coefficient (R2), kinetics of drug release was first order [55].Usually, drugs that are injected into the body follow this kinetics. Based on Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Mt/M∞ = Ktn), where, Mt is the amount of released drug at “t”, M∞ is the amount of released drug at “∞”and k is the release rate constant and n is the release exponent. The values of n were obtained from the slope of the logarithmic curves, which showed that the diffusion and swelling mechanism controlled the drug release rate (non-Fickian or anomalous diffusion).

After 48 h of incubation, the MTT assay displayed that the synthesized CDs were highly compatible against HeLa cells. Cell viability 97–88 % was observed at the range of concentration (4–32 ppm) (Fig. 11 ).

With attachment of CS to CDs, biocompatibility was increased compared to bare CDs. Free DOX significantly prevents the cell growth, and the cell viability of HeLa cells reduced to nearly 40 %. The DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA significantly prevent the cell viability which was more than free DOX. Cancerous cells were targeted by folic acid, leading to less damage of non-cancerous cells, therefore, increasing the cytotoxicity of the DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA on HeLa cells compared with free DOX.

CDs are used for biological imaging due to their non-toxic nature, good fluorescence properties and high water solubility [56]. Fig. 12a and b displays cellular imaging comparison of CDs/CS and CDs/CS-FA. Cellular imaging shows that the CDs/CS-FA clearly introduced to HeLa cells and showed a green fluorescence.

But bare CDs/CS without FA was treated with HeLa cells, the negligible fluorescence was observed, which was due to background noise. These results are due to the important role of FA in cell internalization. As discussed before, many folic acid receptors were dispersed on HeLa cells, which are usually used in drug targeting. Therefore, CDs/CS without FA could not be internalized to HeLa cells.

In summary, the fluorescent CDs were prepared from chitosan solution when subjected to autoclave heating and furthermore conjugated with CS to create a new fluorescent nanocarrier. Folic acid, as cell targeting agent, was loaded onto synthesized CDs/CS. The CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier showed excellent aqueous dispersion stability. The DOX was successfully loaded for the in vitro targeted delivery applications. The optimum DOX loading efficiency and release data of DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier were determined. The results indicate that pH value of CDs/CS-FA solution affect the loading and release of DOX. The profiles of DOX release showed slow and pH dependent release pattern and was found to follow first order kinetics. Treatment of HeLa cells with DOX loaded CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier showed that the efficacy of DOX in biological environment was sufficient and was even more effective than the free DOX treatment. Based on the obtained results, it was found that synthesized CDs/CS-FA nanocarrier has good characteristics such as high drug loading efficiency, low cytotoxicity and desirable cell compatibility. This nanocarrier is suitable candidate for pH responsive drug delivery and cellular imaging.

The undersigned authors transfer all copyright ownership of this manuscript to the International Journal of Biological Macromolecules, in the event the work is published. The undersigned authors warrant the article is original, does not infringe upon any copyright or other proprietary right of any third party, is not under consideration for publication by any other journal, and has not been published previously. The authors confirm that they have reviewed and approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Folic acid based carbon dot functionalized stearic acid-g-polyethyleneimine amphiphilic nanomicelle: targeted drug delivery and imaging for triple negative breast cancer
Colloids Surf. B: Biointerfaces
